misconceptionSummary: Students confuse tenths with tens, or hundredths with hundreds.

steps.narration: The decimal point is the dividing line. Everything left is whole numbers. Everything right is fractions of one.
Each step to the right divides by ten again. Tenths, hundredths, thousandths — notice these names mirror tens, hundreds, thousands on the left, but they are fractions, not whole-number multiples.
Same rule as whole-number place value: digit times column value gives the contribution. Expanding shows exactly what each digit adds to the total.
Six hundredths. The column it sits in — second right of the decimal point — determines the value completely.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: calling the first right column 'tens' instead of 'tenths'. The decimal point separates whole columns from fraction columns.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Tenths and tens sound similar but are completely different. One is to the right of the decimal point, one is to the left.
